CVE-2013-0249.patch_commit_message:
Curl_sasl_create_digest_md5_message: fix buffer overflow

When negotiating SASL DIGEST-MD5 authentication, the function
Curl_sasl_create_digest_md5_message() uses the data provided from the
server without doing the proper length checks and that data is then
appended to a local fixed-size buffer on the stack.

This vulnerability can be exploited by someone who is in control of a
server that a libcurl based program is accessing with POP3, SMTP or
IMAP. For applications that accept user provided URLs, it is also
thinkable that a malicious user would feed an application with a URL to
a server hosting code targetting this flaw.

Bug: http://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_20130206.html

behavior_changes: URI construction: changed from strcpy(uri, service); strcat(uri, "/"); strcat(uri, realm); to snprintf(uri, sizeof(uri), "%s/%s", service, realm); ensuring output fits in 128 bytes.

Response message construction: replaced multiple strcpy/strcat calls building the response string into response[512] with a single snprintf(response, sizeof(response), ...) that formats all fields together, guaranteeing the result does not exceed buffer capacity.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Memory safety: all writes to stack buffers are bounded by their declared sizes, eliminating buffer overflows.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: Replace unsafe string concatenation with bounded snprintf calls that enforce buffer size limits, preventing buffer overflows.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The function Curl_sasl_create_digest_md5_message uses strcpy/strcat to build authentication strings from server-supplied data (realm, nonce, etc.) into fixed-size stack buffers (uri[128], response[512]) without verifying that the total length fits, enabling a stack-based buffer overflow.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: Unbounded strcpy/strcat concatenation of externally controlled data into fixed-size stack buffers without length checks.

CVE-2013-0249.vulnerability_description: Stack-based buffer overflow in the Curl_sasl_create_digest_md5_message function in lib/curl_sasl.c in curl and libcurl 7.26.0 through 7.28.1, when negotiating SASL DIGEST-MD5 authentication, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via a long string in the realm parameter in a (1) POP3, (2) SMTP or (3) IMAP message.

CVE-2013-1944.patch_commit_message:
cookie: fix tailmatching to prevent cross-domain leakage

Cookies set for 'example.com' could accidentaly also be sent by libcurl
to the 'bexample.com' (ie with a prefix to the first domain name).

This is a security vulnerabilty, CVE-2013-1944.

Bug: http://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_20130412.html

new_lines: static bool tailmatch(const char *cooke_domain, const char *hostname)
/* A lead char of cookie_domain is not '.'.
For example, if the value of the Domain attribute is
"example.com", the user agent will include the cookie in the Cookie
header when making HTTP requests to example.com, www.example.com, and
old_lines: static bool tailmatch(const char *little, const char *bigone)

behavior_changes: Added length check: if hostname is shorter than cookie domain, return FALSE.
Added suffix match check with explicit FALSE return if mismatch.
Added exact match check: if hostname length equals cookie domain length, return TRUE.
Added dot-boundary check: if the character immediately before the matched suffix is a dot, return TRUE; otherwise return FALSE.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Ensure cookies are only sent to exact domain matches or proper subdomains, not to domains that merely contain the cookie domain as a suffix.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: The patch enforces RFC 6265 domain matching by requiring either an exact hostname match or the preceding character to be a dot, preventing cross-domain cookie leakage.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The tailmatch function performed only a suffix comparison without verifying that the match occurred at a domain label boundary, allowing cookies set for a domain (e.g., example.com) to be incorrectly sent to sibling domains with a matching suffix (e.g., bexample.com).
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: The old code compared the cookie domain as a suffix of the hostname using Curl_raw_equal without checking if the matched portion was preceded by a dot or if the lengths were equal, violating same-origin policy for cookies.

CVE-2013-1944.vulnerability_description: The tailMatch function in cookie.c in cURL and libcurl before 7.30.0 does not properly match the path domain when sending cookies, which allows remote attackers to steal cookies via a matching suffix in the domain of a URL.

CVE-2014-0015.patch_commit_message:
ConnectionExists: fix NTLM check for new connection

When the requested authentication bitmask includes NTLM, we cannot
re-use a connection for another username/password as we then risk
re-using NTLM (connection-based auth).

This has the unfortunate downside that if you include NTLM as a possible
auth, you cannot re-use connections for other usernames/passwords even
if NTLM doesn't end up the auth type used.

Reported-by: Paras S
Patched-by: Paras S
Bug: http://curl.haxx.se/mail/lib-2014-01/0046.html

behavior_changes: The `wantNTLM` flag is now set to true if the NTLM or NTLM_WB bits are set in the `authhost.want` bitmask, regardless of other bits.
When `wantNTLM` is true, the connection-reuse logic will enforce credential matching (user and password) for HTTP+NTLM connections, preventing cross-user authentication.
The patch does not change the functional behavior when NTLM is the sole authentication method; it only extends the check to multi-auth scenarios.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Prevent unauthorized connection reuse: when NTLM authentication is possible, a connection must not be shared between requests with different user credentials.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: Change the `wantNTLM` computation to use bitwise AND so that it correctly detects the presence of NTLM (or NTLM_WB) in the authentication bitmask, ensuring that NTLM connection reuse is guarded by credential checks.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The `wantNTLM` flag was incorrectly computed using equality checks (`==`) against the entire authentication bitmask instead of bitwise AND (`&`). When NTLM was enabled alongside other authentication methods, `wantNTLM` evaluated to false, allowing connection reuse without verifying user credentials for connections with active NTLM state.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: The pre-patch code uses `data->state.authhost.want == CURLAUTH_NTLM` and `== CURLAUTH_NTLM_WB`, which only matches when the want bitmask consists solely of that flag. With multiple auth methods, the bitmask includes ORed bits, so the equality fails, causing `wantNTLM` to be false. This skips the credential-matching logic for NTLM connections.

CVE-2014-0015.vulnerability_description: cURL and libcurl 7.10.6 through 7.34.0, when more than one authentication method is enabled, re-uses NTLM connections, which might allow context-dependent attackers to authenticate as other users via a request.

CVE-2014-2522.patch_commit_message:
winssl: Enable hostname verification of IP address using SAN or CN

Original commit message was:
 Don't omit CN verification in SChannel when an IP address is used.

Side-effect of this change:
 SChannel and CryptoAPI do not support the iPAddress subjectAltName
 according to RFC 2818. If present, SChannel will first compare the
 IP address to the dNSName subjectAltNames and then fallback to the
 most specific Common Name in the Subject field of the certificate.

 This means that after this change curl will not connect to SSL/TLS
 hosts as long as the IP address is not specified in the SAN or CN
 of the server certificate or the verifyhost option is disabled.

old_lines: infof(data, "schannel: using IP address, SNI is being disabled by "

new_lines: /* Warn if SNI is disabled due to use of an IP address */
infof(data, "schannel: using IP address, SNI is not supported by OS.\n");

behavior_changes: Old: If the hostname was an IP address, server name verification was skipped entirely, and a log message indicated SNI and server name check were disabled.

New: No special handling for IP addresses that disables verification; the hostname (including IP string) is always verified against the certificate. A log message only warns that SNI is not supported by the OS for IP addresses, but verification is not disabled.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Server hostname verification against the certificate's CN or SAN is enforced for every connection, including those using IP addresses, to ensure the certificate matches the intended server identity.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: Remove the automatic disabling of hostname verification for numerical IP addresses, so that certificate subject name checks are always enforced unless manually overridden by the user (verifyhost=0).

root_cause_analysis.summary: When the server hostname was a numerical IP address, the SChannel/WinSSL TLS backend unconditionally disabled server name verification by setting the SCH_CRED_NO_SERVERNAME_CHECK flag. This allowed any valid certificate to be accepted, enabling man-in-the-middle attacks.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: The flag SCH_CRED_NO_SERVERNAME_CHECK was added to schannel_cred.dwFlags whenever Curl_inet_pton() detected an IPv4 or IPv6 address, completely bypassing hostname verification against the certificate's Subject CN or SAN.

CVE-2014-2522.vulnerability_description: curl and libcurl 7.27.0 through 7.35.0, when running on Windows and using the SChannel/Winssl TLS backend, does not verify that the server hostname matches a domain name in the subject's Common Name (CN) or subjectAltName field of the X.509 certificate when accessing a URL that uses a numerical IP address, which allows man-in-the-middle attackers to spoof servers via an arbitrary valid certificate.

CVE-2014-3613.patch_commit_message:
cookies: only use full host matches for hosts used as IP address

By not detecting and rejecting domain names for partial literal IP
addresses properly when parsing received HTTP cookies, libcurl can be
fooled to both send cookies to wrong sites and to allow arbitrary sites
to set cookies for others.

CVE-2014-3613

Bug: http://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_20140910A.html

new_lines: * Return true if the given string is an IP(v4|v6) address.
/* domain name given as IP address */
co->tailmatch=TRUE; /* we always do that if the domain name was
old_lines: co->tailmatch=TRUE; /* we always do that if the domain name was

new_lines: /* check if host is an IP(v4|v6) address */

behavior_changes: In Curl_cookie_add, when the domain is an IP address, the cookie is only stored if the host matches the domain exactly (using strcmp) instead of tailmatch.
tailmatch flag is set to TRUE only for non-IP domains, preventing IP-labeled cookies from later undergoing tailmatch comparisons.
In Curl_cookie_getlist, when the target host is an IP address, tailmatch logic is bypassed entirely; only cookies with an exact domain match are sent.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Cookies set with an IP address domain must only be accepted and sent when the host exactly matches that IP, preventing partial IP address suffix exploits.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: The patch introduces IP address detection (via isip()) and modifies cookie domain acceptance and retrieval to require exact host matches for IP addresses, eliminating tailmatch-based leakage.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The pre-patch code did not differentiate between IP addresses and domain names when applying tailmatch logic for cookies. This allowed partial IP address suffix matching (e.g., a cookie domain '.168.0.1' would match both 192.168.0.1 and 127.168.0.1), enabling cross-site cookie injection and leakage.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: Absence of IP address detection caused the tailmatch function to be applied to IP address strings, allowing suffix-based matching instead of requiring exact full-string equality.

CVE-2014-3613.vulnerability_description: cURL and libcurl before 7.38.0 does not properly handle IP addresses in cookie domain names, which allows remote attackers to set cookies for or send arbitrary cookies to certain sites, as demonstrated by a site at 192.168.0.1 setting cookies for a site at 127.168.0.1.

CVE-2014-3620.patch_commit_message:
cookies: reject incoming cookies set for TLDs

Test 61 was modified to verify this.

CVE-2014-3620

Reported-by: Tim Ruehsen
URL: http://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_20140910B.html

behavior_changes: Previously, cookies with domain names lacking a dot were accepted. Now they are rejected by setting an invalid domain placeholder (':'), causing the cookie to be marked as bad.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Prevent cookie injection for top-level domains, enforcing same-origin policy by requiring at least one embedded dot in the domain attribute.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: Add a check that rejects cookies where the domain string does not contain a dot, preventing setting of cookies for top-level domains.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The function Curl_cookie_add did not reject cookies with domain values lacking an embedded dot (i.e., top-level domains). This allowed setting cookies for TLDs, bypassing the same-origin policy.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: Missing validation of the domain attribute: if the domain string contained no dot (e.g., '.com' or 'com'), the cookie would be accepted without checking for a valid subdomain, leading to overly permissive cookie setting.

CVE-2014-3620.vulnerability_description: cURL and libcurl before 7.38.0 allow remote attackers to bypass the Same Origin Policy and set cookies for arbitrary sites by setting a cookie for a top-level domain.

CVE-2015-3144.patch_commit_message:
fix_hostname: zero length host name caused -1 index offset

If a URL is given with a zero-length host name, like in "http://:80" or
just ":80", `fix_hostname()` will index the host name pointer with a -1
offset (as it blindly assumes a non-zero length) and both read and
assign that address.

CVE-2015-3144

Bug: http://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_20150422D.html
Reported-by: Hanno Böck

behavior_changes: Before patch: `if(host->name[len-1] == '.')` executed unconditionally; with `len=0` it accessed `host->name[-1]`, causing an out-of-bounds read/write.
After patch: `if(len && (host->name[len-1] == '.'))` first checks that `len` is non-zero; if `len` is 0, the condition short-circuits to false, skipping the trailing dot removal entirely.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Ensure that no out-of-bounds memory access occurs when processing hostnames, specifically preventing index underflow for empty hostnames.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: Add a length check (`len && ...`) to prevent the out-of-bounds access when the hostname is zero-length.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The fix_hostname function failed to handle a zero-length hostname, causing an underflow when computing the index `len-1` (where `len = 0`), leading to an out-of-bounds memory access.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: Unconditional access to `host->name[len-1]` without checking whether `len` is greater than zero. When the hostname is empty, `len` is 0, and `len-1` underflows to a very large value (effectively -1), resulting in a read from and write to memory before the start of the name buffer.

CVE-2015-3144.vulnerability_description: The fix_hostname function in cURL and libcurl 7.37.0 through 7.41.0 does not properly calculate an index, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds read or write and crash) or possibly have other unspecified impact via a zero-length host name, as demonstrated by "http://:80" and ":80."

CVE-2015-3145.patch_commit_message:
cookie: cookie parser out of boundary memory access

The internal libcurl function called sanitize_cookie_path() that cleans
up the path element as given to it from a remote site or when read from
a file, did not properly validate the input. If given a path that
consisted of a single double-quote, libcurl would index a newly
allocated memory area with index -1 and assign a zero to it, thus
destroying heap memory it wasn't supposed to.

CVE-2015-3145

Bug: http://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_20150422C.html
Reported-by: Hanno Böck

behavior_changes: Initialize 'len' before any modification to capture the original length, and update it after shifting the string to remove leading quote.
Guard the trailing quote removal with a check that 'len' is non-zero before accessing new_path[len-1].
Change trailing slash removal condition from '1 < len' to 'len' (non-zero) to avoid potential issues with empty strings and improve correctness.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Memory safety: prevent writes outside the allocated buffer bounds by guarding index calculations with length checks.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: The patch computes the length of the path string once, updates it after modifications, and adds checks to ensure the length is positive before performing index-based operations, preventing out-of-bounds accesses.

root_cause_analysis.summary: In sanitize_cookie_path(), when the input cookie_path is a single double-quote character, the code removes the leading quote via memmove, which leaves an empty string. Subsequently, it computes an index as strlen(new_path) - 1, which evaluates to (size_t)-1 (a huge positive value) and writes a NUL byte out-of-bounds, corrupting heap memory.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: Use of unsigned integer arithmetic (strlen - 1) without verifying that strlen > 0, resulting in an out-of-bounds write to memory before the allocated buffer.

CVE-2015-3145.vulnerability_description: The sanitize_cookie_path function in cURL and libcurl 7.31.0 through 7.41.0 does not properly calculate an index, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds write and crash) or possibly have other unspecified impact via a cookie path containing only a double-quote character.

CVE-2015-3153.patch_commit_message:
CURLOPT_HEADEROPT: default to separate

Make the HTTP headers separated by default for improved security and
reduced risk for information leakage.

Bug: http://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_20150429.html
Reported-by: Yehezkel Horowitz, Oren Souroujon

new_lines: set->sep_headers = TRUE; /* separated header lists by default */

behavior_changes: Before patch: Custom headers set with CURLOPT_HTTPHEADER were sent to both proxy and destination server by default.
After patch: Custom headers are only sent to the destination server by default. Applications needing the old behavior must set CURLOPT_HEADEROPT to CURLHEADER_UNIFIED.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Confidentiality of custom HTTP headers when using an HTTP proxy, ensuring they are not inadvertently disclosed to the proxy.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: The patch sets `sep_headers` to TRUE in `Curl_init_userdefined`, making separate header lists the default. This prevents custom headers from being sent to proxies unless the application explicitly requests unified mode via `CURLOPT_HEADEROPT`.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The default configuration for handling custom HTTP headers in libcurl did not separate headers destined for the proxy from those for the destination server. The internal flag `sep_headers` was not initialized to TRUE, so it defaulted to FALSE (unified mode), causing all custom headers to be sent to both the proxy and the server, potentially leaking sensitive information to the proxy.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: The `sep_headers` field in `struct UserDefined` was not explicitly set to TRUE during initialization, so it defaulted to FALSE. As a result, custom headers provided via `CURLOPT_HTTPHEADER` were forwarded to HTTP proxies in requests such as CONNECT, exposing header contents.

CVE-2015-3153.vulnerability_description: The default configuration for cURL and libcurl before 7.42.1 sends custom HTTP headers to both the proxy and destination server, which might allow remote proxy servers to obtain sensitive information by reading the header contents.

CVE-2015-3237.patch_commit_message:
SMB: rangecheck values read off incoming packet

CVE-2015-3237

Detected by Coverity. CID 1299430.

Bug: http://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_20150617B.html

behavior_changes: Before patch: The offset and length from the SMB response were used directly to compute the data pointer and length for Curl_client_write, with no validation against the actual received data size.

After patch: If off + sizeof(unsigned int) + len exceeds smbc->got, an error CURLE_RECV_ERROR is returned and the invalid data is not passed to Curl_client_write. Otherwise, the write proceeds as before.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Input validation to ensure memory access stays within the bounds of the received SMB packet.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: Add a bounds check to ensure that the sum of the offset, size of an unsigned int, and length does not exceed the total received packet size, preventing out-of-bounds access.

root_cause_analysis.summary: In smb_request_state, the length and offset values parsed from SMB responses were used without bounds checking when calling Curl_client_write, allowing a malicious server to cause an out-of-bounds read by providing crafted length and offset fields.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: The pointer (char *)msg + off + sizeof(unsigned int) with length len is passed to Curl_client_write without verifying that the memory region lies within the received packet (msg with size smbc->got).

CVE-2015-3237.vulnerability_description: The smb_request_state function in cURL and libcurl 7.40.0 through 7.42.1 allows remote SMB servers to obtain sensitive information from memory or cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds read and crash) via crafted length and offset values.

CVE-2016-3739.patch_commit_message:
mbedtls/polarssl: set "hostname" unconditionally

...as otherwise the TLS libs will skip the CN/SAN check and just allow
connection to any server. curl previously skipped this function when SNI
wasn't used or when connecting to an IP address specified host.

CVE-2016-3739

Bug: https://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_20160518A.html
Reported-by: Moti Avrahami

new_lines: /* mbedtls_ssl_set_hostname() sets the name to use in CN/SAN checks *and*
the name to set in the SNI extension. So even if curl connects to a
host specified as an IP address, this function must be used. */
/* ssl_set_hostname() sets the name to use in CN/SAN checks *and* the name
to set in the SNI extension. So even if curl connects to a host
specified as an IP address, this function must be used. */

behavior_changes: Previously: ssl_set_hostname() was called only if the hostname was not an IP address and SNI was enabled; failure only logged a warning.
Now: ssl_set_hostname() is called unconditionally; failure is a hard error, returning CURLE_SSL_CONNECT_ERROR and aborting the connection.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Consistent server certificate hostname validation for all TLS/SSL connections, preventing impersonation via arbitrary valid certificates.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: Remove the conditional checks and always call ssl_set_hostname(). Treat failure as a hard error, aborting the connection. This ensures that hostname verification is performed for all connections, including IP addresses and SSLv3.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The mbedtls/polarssl backend did not call ssl_set_hostname() when connecting to an IP address or when SNI was disabled (e.g., SSLv3). This caused the TLS library to skip hostname verification against the certificate CN/SAN, allowing an attacker to impersonate a server with any valid certificate.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: The call to mbedtls_ssl_set_hostname() / ssl_set_hostname() was guarded by conditions: only if the hostname was not an IP address (checked via Curl_inet_pton) and SNI was enabled. If these conditions were false, the function was skipped, omitting critical hostname verification.

CVE-2016-3739.vulnerability_description: The (1) mbed_connect_step1 function in lib/vtls/mbedtls.c and (2) polarssl_connect_step1 function in lib/vtls/polarssl.c in cURL and libcurl before 7.49.0, when using SSLv3 or making a TLS connection to a URL that uses a numerical IP address, allow remote attackers to spoof servers via an arbitrary valid certificate.

CVE-2016-8617.patch_commit_message:
base64: check for integer overflow on large input

CVE-2016-8617

Bug: https://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_20161102C.html
Reported-by: Cure53

behavior_changes: On 32-bit systems, if insize > UINT_MAX/4, the function now returns CURLE_OUT_OF_MEMORY instead of performing the vulnerable allocation.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Prevent heap buffer overflow by ensuring the allocation size calculation does not overflow.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: The patch adds a size check for 32-bit systems: if `insize > UINT_MAX/4`, it returns `CURLE_OUT_OF_MEMORY` to prevent integer overflow in the subsequent allocation size calculation.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The base64_encode function allocated output buffer using `malloc(insize * 4 / 3 + 4)`. On 32-bit systems, large insize values (> UINT_MAX/4) caused the multiplication to overflow, resulting in a too-small allocation and subsequent heap buffer overflow when encoding data.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: Integer overflow in size calculation `insize * 4 / 3 + 4` due to lack of bounds check on `insize` prior to multiplication, leading to heap buffer overflow.

CVE-2016-8617.vulnerability_description: The base64 encode function in curl before version 7.51.0 is prone to a buffer being under allocated in 32bit systems if it receives at least 1Gb as input via `CURLOPT_USERNAME`.

CVE-2016-8618.patch_commit_message:
aprintf: detect wrap-around when growing allocation

On 32bit systems we could otherwise wrap around after 2GB and allocate 0
bytes and crash.

CVE-2016-8618

Bug: https://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_20161102D.html
Reported-by: Cure53

new_lines: /* detect wrap-around or other overflow problems */

behavior_changes: Old behavior: unconditionally computed `infop->alloc*2` and called realloc with that size, then updated `infop->alloc` to the potentially wrapped value, risking a double-free.

New behavior: computes the new size and checks for wrap-around (`newsize > infop->alloc`). If no overflow, performs realloc; otherwise, sets failure and returns -1 without altering the buffer or the allocation size.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Prevent memory corruption from integer overflow by ensuring allocation sizes never wrap to a smaller value, thereby avoiding dangerous realloc(ptr, 0) calls.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: The patch adds a detection for integer wrap-around when computing the new allocation size. Before calling realloc, it verifies that `newsize > infop->alloc`. If overflow is detected, the reallocation is skipped and the function returns failure, preventing memory corruption.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The function alloc_addbyter doubled the allocation size by performing `infop->alloc*2` without checking for integer overflow. On 32-bit systems, when `infop->alloc` reached 2GB (0x80000000), the multiplication wrapped to 0, causing `realloc(ptr, 0)` which freed the buffer while leaving a dangling pointer, leading to a double-free.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: Unchecked size_t multiplication that wraps around on 32-bit platforms, passing a zero size to realloc and freeing the buffer without updating the pointer, resulting in a dangling pointer and subsequent double-free.

CVE-2016-8618.vulnerability_description: The libcurl API function called `curl_maprintf()` before version 7.51.0 can be tricked into doing a double-free due to an unsafe `size_t` multiplication, on systems using 32 bit `size_t` variables.

CVE-2016-8619.patch_commit_message:
krb5: avoid realloc(0)

If the requested size is zero, bail out with error instead of doing a
realloc() that would cause a double-free: realloc(0) acts as a free()
and then there's a second free in the cleanup path.

CVE-2016-8619

Bug: https://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_20161102E.html
Reported-by: Cure53

new_lines: /* only realloc if there was a length */

behavior_changes: Previously, if len was zero, realloc(buf->data, 0) freed buf->data and set tmp to NULL, then the error path returned without altering buf->data, causing a double-free later.

Now, if len is zero, realloc is not called, tmp remains NULL, the function returns error immediately, and buf->data is left unmodified and still points to valid memory (if any), preventing the double-free.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Memory safety: eliminate double-free by ensuring that realloc(ptr, 0) is never called, and that buf->data is not freed unexpectedly before cleanup.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: Prevent the double-free by skipping the realloc call when the requested size is zero, thus preserving the original buf->data pointer and avoiding the premature free.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The function read_data() could receive a zero length from the network, leading to realloc(ptr, 0) which frees the old buffer. If the function then returns an error without updating buf->data, the old pointer remains in buf->data but the memory is already freed, causing a double-free when cleanup later calls free(buf->data).
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: Calling realloc(buf->data, 0) when len is zero, which acts as free(buf->data) and returns NULL, but the function does not update buf->data to NULL before returning error, leaving it as a dangling pointer that is freed again in cleanup.

CVE-2016-8619.vulnerability_description: The function `read_data()` in security.c in curl before version 7.51.0 is vulnerable to memory double free.

CVE-2016-8621.patch_commit_message:
parsedate: handle cut off numbers better

... and don't read outside of the given buffer!

CVE-2016-8621

bug: https://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_20161102G.html
Reported-by: Luật Nguyễn

behavior_changes: For HH:MM:SS parsing, old code: date += 8; new code: date += len, where len is captured via '%02d:%02d:%02d%n'.
For HH:MM parsing, old code: date += 5; new code: date += len, where len is captured via '%02d:%02d%n'.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Memory safety: prevent out-of-bounds reads by keeping the parse pointer within the valid input string.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: Replace the hardcoded pointer advancement with the exact number of characters parsed, as reported by the %n format specifier in sscanf, to ensure the pointer never moves past the input buffer.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The function hardcoded pointer increments (date += 8 or date += 5) after sscanf without verifying the actual number of characters consumed, causing the pointer to advance beyond the input string when the matched portion was shorter than expected, leading to an out-of-bounds read.

  was checked to see if it ended with the modified cert name.

patch_hunk.header: @@ -1602,14 +1603,9 @@ static CURLcode verify_certificate(struct connectdata *conn, int sockindex)
@@ -1620,31 +1616,50 @@ static CURLcode verify_certificate(struct connectdata *conn, int sockindex)
new_lines: /* Comparing the cert name and the connection hostname encoded as UTF-8
* is acceptable since both values are assumed to use ASCII
old_lines: /* this is a wildcard cert.  try matching the last len - 1 chars */

CVE-2017-2629.patch_commit_message:
TLS: make SSL_VERIFYSTATUS work again

The CURLOPT_SSL_VERIFYSTATUS option was not properly handled by libcurl
and thus even if the status couldn't be verified, the connection would
be allowed and the user would not be told about the failed verification.

Regression since cb4e2be7c6d42ca

CVE-2017-2629
Bug: https://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_20170222.html

Reported-by: Marcus Hoffmann

behavior_changes: Before patch: `conn->ssl_config.verifystatus` (and proxy) remained uninitialized (defaulting to 0, meaning verification disabled), even when the user set `CURLOPT_SSL_VERIFYSTATUS` to 1.

After patch: The connection's `verifystatus` flag is explicitly set from the user's option, so the TLS layer will request and verify certificate status, and the connection will succeed only if a valid proof is presented.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: When `CURLOPT_SSL_VERIFYSTATUS` is enabled, the TLS handshake must correctly validate the OCSP stapling response, causing the connection to fail if the server certificate status cannot be verified or is invalid.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: Explicitly set `conn->ssl_config.verifystatus` and `conn->proxy_ssl_config.verifystatus` from the user configuration during connection allocation, ensuring that the requested TLS certificate status verification is actually performed.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The `allocate_conn` function failed to copy the user-set `verifystatus` flag from `data->set.ssl.primary.verifystatus` to the connection's `ssl_config.verifystatus` (and similarly for proxy SSL). As a result, the TLS Certificate Status Request (OCSP stapling) verification was never triggered, causing the connection to succeed even when no valid proof existed.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: Missing assignment of `conn->ssl_config.verifystatus` from the user configuration, causing the verification logic to treat the option as disabled and skip all certificate status checks.

CVE-2017-2629.vulnerability_description: curl before 7.53.0 has an incorrect TLS Certificate Status Request extension feature that asks for a fresh proof of the server's certificate's validity in the code that checks for a test success or failure. It ends up always thinking there's valid proof, even when there is none or if the server doesn't support the TLS extension in question. This could lead to users not detecting when a server's certificate goes invalid or otherwise be mislead that the server is in a better shape than it is in reality. This flaw also exists in the command line tool (--cert-status).

It now computes the total allocation size by adding explicit padding to align the struct's end to a 32-byte boundary, then adds the full size of the four ssl_backend_data objects. Pointers to the backend data are set relative to the end of the allocation, ensuring correct size and alignment.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The allocate_conn function miscalculated the memory required for the connectdata structure on 32-bit platforms. The pre-patch code subtracted sizeof(long long) (8 bytes) from the SSL backend data allocation to account for an alignment field, but that field was incorrectly declared as a pointer (long long *) of only 4 bytes. This caused the total allocation to be 4 bytes too small, leading to out-of-bounds reads or writes when accessing the last SSL backend data structure.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: The macro SSL_EXTRA was defined as '4 * sizeof_ssl_backend_data - sizeof(long long)', subtracting 8 bytes based on a mistaken assumption that the alignment field was a long long (8 bytes). The actual field was a long long * (4 bytes on 32-bit), so the allocation was short by 4 bytes.

CVE-2017-8818.vulnerability_description: curl and libcurl before 7.57.0 on 32-bit platforms allow attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds access and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact because too little memory is allocated for interfacing to an SSL library.

CVE-2017-9502.patch_commit_message:
url: fix buffer overwrite with file protocol (CVE-2017-9502)

Bug: https://github.com/curl/curl/issues/1540
Advisory: https://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_20170614.html

Assisted-by: Ray Satiro
Reported-by: Marcel Raad

new_lines: /* only file: is allowed to omit one or both slashes */
old_lines: size_t urllen = strlen(data->change.url); /* original URL length */

behavior_changes: `*prot_missing` is now set to `!url_has_scheme` inside the file protocol handler, correctly reflecting that a protocol prefix must be prepended when the URL has no scheme.

Prefix length calculation now verifies that the protocol name in `protop` matches the beginning of the URL and handles slashes conditionally: for `file:`, the length accounts for only the actual number of slashes present (0, 1, or 2); for other protocols, exactly two slashes are required or the URL is considered malformed.
Buffer allocation changed from `malloc(urllen + 2)` to `malloc(prefixlen + plen + 1)`, ensuring sufficient space for the reconstructed URL.
Error handling added: if the URL prefix does not match the expected protocol string, the function fails with `CURLE_URL_MALFORMAT`.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Memory safety: prevent heap buffer overflow during URL reconstruction for file:// protocols on Windows/DOS when drive letters are used.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: Ensure `*prot_missing` is true for file schemes without an explicit scheme in the URL, calculate the prefix length dynamically by inspecting the actual URL prefix (handling file: with optional slashes), and allocate the rebuilt URL buffer with the correct total size (`prefixlen + plen + 1`), thereby preventing the heap overflow.

root_cause_analysis.summary: When a file:// URL with a Windows/DOS drive letter lacked the scheme or had missing slashes, the protocol-missing flag (`*prot_missing`) was not set correctly, causing the URL rebuilding logic to always assume a `file://` prefix of 7 bytes. This led to a miscalculation of the buffer size (allocated as `urllen+2`) and a subsequent heap buffer overflow when copying the path, because the written data exceeded the allocated space by up to 7 bytes.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: Heap buffer overflow due to insufficient allocation: `reurl = malloc(urllen + 2)` did not account for the actual prefix length, and `prefixlen` was incorrectly extended by a fixed 7 bytes (`strlen(protop) + strlen("://")`) regardless of whether the original URL contained those bytes, causing `memcpy` to write beyond the buffer bounds when the path was appended.

CVE-2017-9502.vulnerability_description: In curl before 7.54.1 on Windows and DOS, libcurl's default protocol function, which is the logic that allows an application to set which protocol libcurl should attempt to use when given a URL without a scheme part, had a flaw that could lead to it overwriting a heap based memory buffer with seven bytes. If the default protocol is specified to be FILE or a file: URL lacks two slashes, the given "URL" starts with a drive letter, and libcurl is built for Windows or DOS, then libcurl would copy the path 7 bytes off, so that the end of the given path would write beyond the malloc buffer (7 bytes being the length in bytes of the ascii string "file://").

CVE-2018-0500.patch_commit_message:
smtp: use the upload buffer size for scratch buffer malloc

... not the read buffer size, as that can be set smaller and thus cause
a buffer overflow! CVE-2018-0500

Reported-by: Peter Wu
Bug: https://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_2018-70a2.html

patch_hunk.header: @@ -1563,13 +1563,14 @@ CURLcode Curl_smtp_escape_eob(struct connectdata *conn, const ssize_t nread)

behavior_changes: malloc(2 * data->set.buffer_size) → malloc(2 * UPLOAD_BUFSIZE)
Added DEBUGASSERT(UPLOAD_BUFSIZE >= nread) to catch logic errors.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Memory safety: prevent heap buffer overflow by allocating sufficient space based on the actual maximum data size processed, not an unrelated, potentially smaller configuration.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: Fix the allocation to always use UPLOAD_BUFSIZE instead of the user‑configurable read buffer size, ensuring the scratch buffer is large enough for any possible nread value and its escaped form.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The function Curl_smtp_escape_eob allocated a scratch buffer using the configurable read buffer size (data->set.buffer_size) rather than the fixed upload buffer size (UPLOAD_BUFSIZE). Since nread can be as large as UPLOAD_BUFSIZE, a smaller read buffer size leads to an undersized scratch buffer, causing a heap-based overflow when copying escaped data.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: Use of data->set.buffer_size (may be smaller than UPLOAD_BUFSIZE) to allocate the scratch buffer, insufficient to hold worst-case escaped output for a full upload buffer (at most 2 * UPLOAD_BUFSIZE).

CVE-2018-0500.vulnerability_description: Curl_smtp_escape_eob in lib/smtp.c in curl 7.54.1 to and including curl 7.60.0 has a heap-based buffer overflow that might be exploitable by an attacker who can control the data that curl transmits over SMTP with certain settings (i.e., use of a nonstandard --limit-rate argument or CURLOPT_BUFFERSIZE value).

CVE-2018-1000007.patch_commit_message:
http: prevent custom Authorization headers in redirects

... unless CURLOPT_UNRESTRICTED_AUTH is set to allow them. This matches how
curl already handles Authorization headers created internally.

Note: this changes behavior slightly, for the sake of reducing mistakes.

Added test 317 and 318 to verify.

Reported-by: Craig de Stigter
Bug: https://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_2018-b3bf.html

new_lines: /* be careful of sending this potentially sensitive header to

behavior_changes: Custom `Authorization:` headers are now omitted during redirects unless the target host matches the original `first_host` or the user sets `CURLOPT_UNRESTRICTED_AUTH` (now mapped to `data->set.allow_auth_to_other_hosts`).

The internal option `http_disable_hostname_check_before_authentication` is renamed to `allow_auth_to_other_hosts` and exposed publicly as `CURLOPT_UNRESTRICTED_AUTH`, controlling both internal and custom auth forwarding.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Prevent automatic forwarding of sensitive custom Authorization headers to hosts other than the initial request target during redirects, matching the existing protection for internally generated authentication headers.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: The patch adds a redirect-aware guard in `Curl_add_custom_headers` to suppress custom Authorization headers when following a redirect to a different host, unless the user explicitly allows it via the renamed option `allow_auth_to_other_hosts`.

root_cause_analysis.summary: libcurl prior to fix sent custom Authorization headers to all redirect targets without any hostname validation, causing potential leakage of authentication data to third-party hosts.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: In `Curl_add_custom_headers`, headers matching 'Authorization:' were unconditionally added to the request, even during redirects (`data->state.this_is_a_follow`) when the target host differed from the original first host. There was no check of `data->state.first_host` or `data->set.allow_auth_to_other_hosts`.

CVE-2018-1000007.vulnerability_description: libcurl 7.1 through 7.57.0 might accidentally leak authentication data to third parties. When asked to send custom headers in its HTTP requests, libcurl will send that set of headers first to the host in the initial URL but also, if asked to follow redirects and a 30X HTTP response code is returned, to the host mentioned in URL in the `Location:` response header value. Sending the same set of headers to subsequent hosts is in particular a problem for applications that pass on custom `Authorization:` headers, as this header often contains privacy sensitive information or data that could allow others to impersonate the libcurl-using client's request.

CVE-2018-1000120.patch_commit_message:
FTP: reject path components with control codes

Refuse to operate when given path components featuring byte values lower
than 32.

Previously, inserting a %00 sequence early in the directory part when
using the 'singlecwd' ftp method could make curl write a zero byte
outside of the allocated buffer.

Bug: https://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_2018-9cd6.html

old_lines: result = Curl_urldecode(data, inpath, n, &lstArg, NULL, FALSE);

root_cause_analysis.summary: The FTP path handling in curl allowed URL‑decoded path components to contain control characters (e.g., null bytes from %00), which could lead to a buffer overflow when processing directory components, particularly in the 'singlecwd' FTP method.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: Calls to Curl_urldecode with the last argument set to FALSE permitted decoding of all characters including control codes. This enabled an attacker to inject a null byte early in the path, causing a write outside the allocated buffer.

CVE-2018-1000300.patch_commit_message:
pingpong: fix response cache memcpy overflow

Response data for a handle with a large buffer might be cached and then
used with the "closure" handle when it has a smaller buffer and then the
larger cache will be copied and overflow the new smaller heap based
buffer.

Reported-by: Dario Weisser
CVE: CVE-2018-1000300
Bug: https://curl.haxx.se/docs/adv_2018-82c2.html

behavior_changes: Old: DEBUGASSERT condition had no effect in release builds; memcpy could overflow the buffer if cache_size is too large relative to the buffer position.
New: Runtime if-check redirects to error handling, returning CURLE_RECV_ERROR and logging an error message, thus stopping execution before the overflow occurs.

CVE-2018-1000300.vulnerability_description: curl version curl 7.54.1 to and including curl 7.59.0 contains a CWE-122: Heap-based Buffer Overflow vulnerability in denial of service and more that can result in curl might overflow a heap based memory buffer when closing down an FTP connection with very long server command replies.. CVE-2019-5482.vulnerability_description: Heap buffer overflow in the TFTP protocol handler in cURL 7.19.4 to 7.65.3.

CVE-2020-8169.patch_commit_message:
url: make the updated credentials URL-encoded in the URL

Found-by: Gregory Jefferis
Reported-by: Jeroen Ooms
Added test 1168 to verify. Bug spotted when doing a redirect.
Bug: https://github.com/jeroen/curl/issues/224
Closes #5400

behavior_changes: Added CURLU_URLENCODE flag to curl_url_set for CURLUPART_USER
Added CURLU_URLENCODE flag to curl_url_set for CURLUPART_PASSWORD
Previously, no encoding was applied; now credentials are percent-encoded to escape special characters like '@' and ':'
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Confidentiality of credentials in the URL, preventing leakage of password data to DNS or network logs via malformed URLs.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: Ensure that user and password components are URL-encoded when set into the URL, preventing special characters from being misinterpreted and thus protecting credential confidentiality.

root_cause_analysis.summary: When updating the URL with user credentials, the old code did not specify URL encoding for the user and password parts. Special characters in the password (e.g., '@', ':') could be passed unencoded, causing the URL parser to misinterpret them as host separators, leading to partial password leakage to DNS servers during hostname resolution.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: Missing URL encoding in curl_url_set calls for CURLUPART_USER and CURLUPART_PASSWORD.

CVE-2020-8169.vulnerability_description: curl 7.62.0 through 7.70.0 is vulnerable to an information disclosure vulnerability that can lead to a partial password being leaked over the network and to the DNS server(s).

CVE-2020-8284.patch_commit_message:
ftp: CURLOPT_FTP_SKIP_PASV_IP by default

The command line tool also independently sets --ftp-skip-pasv-ip by
default.

Ten test cases updated to adapt the modified --libcurl output.

Bug: https://curl.se/docs/CVE-2020-8284.html
CVE-2020-8284

Reported-by: Varnavas Papaioannou

new_lines: set->ftp_skip_ip = TRUE;    /* skip PASV IP by default */

behavior_changes: Pre-patch: The FTP data connection would be established to the IP address and port specified in the PASV response from the server.
Post-patch: By default, the IP address in the PASV response is ignored; the client connects to the same IP address as the control connection, using the port from the PASV response.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: The data connection must be established to the same IP address as the control connection, unless the user explicitly overrides this (e.g., via CURLOPT_FTPPORT).
patch_intent_analysis.summary: The patch changes the default behavior to ignore the PASV IP and instead reuse the control connection IP, preventing the server from redirecting the data connection.

root_cause_analysis.summary: curl previously used the IP address from an FTP PASV response to establish the data connection, allowing a malicious server to redirect the connection to an arbitrary internal IP, leading to information disclosure.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: The FTP client would connect to the IP address provided in the PASV response without verifying that it matches the control connection's IP, enabling a server-side request forgery (SSRF) attack.

CVE-2020-8284.vulnerability_description: A malicious server can use the FTP PASV response to trick curl 7.73.0 and earlier into connecting back to a given IP address and port, and this way potentially make curl extract information about services that are otherwise private and not disclosed, for example doing port scanning and service banner extractions.

CVE-2021-22897.patch_commit_message:
schannel: don't use static to store selected ciphers

CVE-2021-22897

Bug: https://curl.se/docs/CVE-2021-22897.html

old_lines: static ALG_ID algIds[45]; /*There are 45 listed in the MS headers*/

behavior_changes: Added a new parameter `int *algIds` to `set_ssl_ciphers` to receive cipher IDs from the caller.
Removed the `static ALG_ID algIds[45]` local variable; now uses the caller-provided array.
Updated the caller in `schannel_connect_step1` to pass `BACKEND->algIds`, a connection-specific buffer.
Changed the loop bound from hardcoded 45 to `NUMOF_CIPHERS` (likely larger to accommodate more ciphers).
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Isolation of cipher suite configurations between concurrent TLS connections.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: Eliminate the static cipher storage by requiring the caller to provide a per-connection buffer, ensuring each TLS session has its own independent cipher list.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The set_ssl_ciphers function used a static local array to store cipher IDs, causing all Schannel TLS sessions to share a single cipher configuration. The last session to call the function would overwrite the shared state, affecting all concurrent transfers.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: Static local variable `static ALG_ID algIds[45]` inside `set_ssl_ciphers` function, used as shared mutable state across all invocations.

CVE-2021-22897.vulnerability_description: curl 7.61.0 through 7.76.1 suffers from exposure of data element to wrong session due to a mistake in the code for CURLOPT_SSL_CIPHER_LIST when libcurl is built to use the Schannel TLS library. The selected cipher set was stored in a single "static" variable in the library, which has the surprising side-effect that if an application sets up multiple concurrent transfers, the last one that sets the ciphers will accidentally control the set used by all transfers. In a worst-case scenario, this weakens transport security significantly.

CVE-2021-22898.patch_commit_message:
telnet: check sscanf() for correct number of matches

CVE-2021-22898

Bug: https://curl.se/docs/CVE-2021-22898.html

behavior_changes: Previously, if sscanf matched only 1 format specifier (i.e., if the input lacked a comma after varname), the code would proceed with an uninitialized varval, leaking stack data. Now, such malformed inputs are ignored.
The patch ensures that only inputs that successfully parse both varname and varval are used to construct the TELNET suboption message.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Prevents disclosure of uninitialized stack memory by ensuring only fully parsed telnet option variables are processed.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: Change the condition to require exactly 2 successful matches, ensuring that both `varname` and `varval` were properly parsed before use.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The vulnerable code used `if(sscanf(v->data, "%127[^,],%127s", varname, varval))` to parse telnet option variables. Because any non-zero return from sscanf was treated as success, a partial match (e.g., only `varname` matched) would leave `varval` uninitialized. The subsequent `msnprintf` would then copy uninitialized stack data, potentially exposing sensitive information.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: Treating a non-zero sscanf return as sufficient validation for full initialization of all destination variables, leading to uninitialized memory copy.

CVE-2021-22898.vulnerability_description: curl 7.7 through 7.76.1 suffers from an information disclosure when the `-t` command line option, known as `CURLOPT_TELNETOPTIONS` in libcurl, is used to send variable=content pairs to TELNET servers. Due to a flaw in the option parser for sending NEW_ENV variables, libcurl could be made to pass on uninitialized data from a stack based buffer to the server, resulting in potentially revealing sensitive internal information to the server using a clear-text network protocol.

openssl: associate/detach the transfer from connection

CVE-2021-22901

behavior_changes: In Curl_attach_connnection, added call to Curl_ssl_associate_conn(data, conn) to register the transfer with the connection right after attachment.
In Curl_detach_connnection, added call to Curl_ssl_detach_conn(data, conn) before clearing data->conn, which triggers clearing of SSL ex_data and session cache invalidation.
In ossl_connect_step1, replaced direct SSL_set_ex_data calls with a call to ossl_associate_connection(data, conn, sockindex) to centralize association logic.
Added ossl_associate_connection function that stores transfer (Curl_easy) and connection (connectdata) pointers in SSL ex_data for use by TLS 1.3 session callbacks.
Added ossl_disassociate_connection function that sets the SSL ex_data pointers to NULL and removes any cached session ID, called when a transfer detaches to prevent use-after-free.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Temporal memory safety (no use-after-free) for transfer objects associated with TLS connections.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: Introduce explicit association and disassociation of transfers with connections, so that when a transfer is detached, the stored pointers in OpenSSL ex_data are cleared and any cached session is invalidated, preventing use-after-free.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The pre-patch code stored pointers to the transfer object (Curl_easy) in OpenSSL ex_data during SSL setup, but did not clear them when the transfer detached from the connection. If the transfer was freed before a TLS 1.3 session ticket callback executed, the callback would access freed memory, leading to use-after-free.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: Storing Curl_easy and connectdata pointers as SSL ex_data via SSL_set_ex_data in ossl_connect_step1 without any corresponding invalidation when the transfer object is detached or freed, allowing the TLS session ticket callback (ossl_new_session_cb) to dereference dangling pointers.

CVE-2021-22901.vulnerability_description: curl 7.75.0 through 7.76.1 suffers from a use-after-free vulnerability resulting in already freed memory being used when a TLS 1.3 session ticket arrives over a connection. A malicious server can use this in rare unfortunate circumstances to potentially reach remote code execution in the client. When libcurl at run-time sets up support for TLS 1.3 session tickets on a connection using OpenSSL, it stores pointers to the transfer in-memory object for later retrieval when a session ticket arrives. If the connection is used by multiple transfers (like with a reused HTTP/1.1 connection or multiplexed HTTP/2 connection) that first transfer object might be freed before the new session is established on that connection and then the function will access a memory buffer that might be freed. When using that memory, libcurl might even call a function pointer in the object, making it possible for a remote code execution if the server could somehow manage to get crafted memory content into the correct place in memory.

CVE-2022-42915.patch_commit_message:
http_proxy: restore the protocol pointer on error

Reported-by: Trail of Bits

Closes #9790

old_lines: /* restore the protocol pointer, if not already done */

patch_hunk.header: @@ -751,15 +751,6 @@ static void conn_shutdown(struct Curl_easy *data, struct connectdata *conn)
old_lines: /* If this was closed with a CONNECT in progress, cleanup this temporary

behavior_changes: In `conn_shutdown`, the block that checked for `prot_save` and called `Curl_safefree` on it while setting `data->req.p.http = NULL` is removed.
In `Curl_connect_done`, the conditional copying of `s->prot_save` to `data->req.p.http` is replaced with an unconditional assignment, and the subsequent `s->prot_save = NULL` is removed.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Memory safety against double free by preventing dangling pointers and ensuring `prot_save` is deallocated only once.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: The patch removes the premature free of `prot_save` in `conn_shutdown` and simplifies the restoration logic in `Curl_connect_done` to unconditionally assign `s->prot_save` to `data->req.p.http` without clearing `s->prot_save`, ensuring the pointer is freed only once through the normal protocol cleanup path.

root_cause_analysis.summary: In the error handling path when an HTTP proxy refuses a CONNECT request for non-HTTP(S) URL schemes, the connection shutdown function `conn_shutdown` prematurely freed the `prot_save` pointer, leaving a dangling pointer in `conn->connect_state->prot_save`. Later, `Curl_connect_done` assigned this dangling pointer to `data->req.p.http`, and a subsequent free during transfer cleanup triggered a double free.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: `conn_shutdown` freed `conn->connect_state->prot_save` without setting the pointer to NULL, allowing `Curl_connect_done` to later retain a dangling reference in `data->req.p.http`, which was then freed again.

CVE-2022-42915.vulnerability_description: curl before 7.86.0 has a double free. If curl is told to use an HTTP proxy for a transfer with a non-HTTP(S) URL, it sets up the connection to the remote server by issuing a CONNECT request to the proxy, and then tunnels the rest of the protocol through. An HTTP proxy might refuse this request (HTTP proxies often only allow outgoing connections to specific port numbers, like 443 for HTTPS) and instead return a non-200 status code to the client. Due to flaws in the error/cleanup handling, this could trigger a double free in curl if one of the following schemes were used in the URL for the transfer: dict, gopher, gophers, ldap, ldaps, rtmp, rtmps, or telnet. The earliest affected version is 7.77.0.

CVE-2022-42916.patch_commit_message:
url: use IDN decoded names for HSTS checks

Reported-by: Hiroki Kurosawa

Closes #9791

new_lines: /* This looks like an IPv6 address literal. See if there is an address
/* make sure the connect struct gets its own copy of the host name */
/* This MUST use the IDN decoded name */

old_lines: /* This looks like an IPv6 address literal. See if there is an address
/* make sure the connect struct gets its own copy of the host name */

behavior_changes: IDN conversion is now performed in parseurlandfillconn() instead of later in create_conn().
The HSTS check now uses conn->host.name (after IDN conversion) instead of data->state.up.hostname.
Hostname copy and IPv6 bracket removal now occur before the HSTS check, in parseurlandfillconn(), to provide the correct hostname for both IDN conversion and HSTS lookup.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: HSTS enforcement for IDN hostnames, preventing protocol downgrade from HTTPS to HTTP by ensuring the HSTS lookup uses the ASCII hostname.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: The patch moves IDN conversion from create_conn() into parseurlandfillconn(), executing it before the HSTS check, and then passes the IDN-decoded hostname (conn->host.name) to Curl_hsts().

root_cause_analysis.summary: The HSTS check was performed using the raw, potentially IDN-encoded hostname from the URL, while the HSTS cache was keyed by the ASCII punycode representation. This mismatch caused HSTS to be bypassed for IDN hostnames, keeping the connection HTTP.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: In parseurlandfillconn(), Curl_hsts() was called with data->state.up.hostname before any IDN conversion, so the lookup used the original URL hostname (e.g., with Unicode characters) instead of the ASCII-compatible encoding stored in the HSTS cache.

CVE-2022-42916.vulnerability_description: In curl before 7.86.0, the HSTS check could be bypassed to trick it into staying with HTTP. Using its HSTS support, curl can be instructed to use HTTPS directly (instead of using an insecure cleartext HTTP step) even when HTTP is provided in the URL. This mechanism could be bypassed if the host name in the given URL uses IDN characters that get replaced with ASCII counterparts as part of the IDN conversion, e.g., using the character UTF-8 U+3002 (IDEOGRAPHIC FULL STOP) instead of the common ASCII full stop of U+002E (.). The earliest affected version is 7.77.0 2021-05-26.

CVE-2023-46218.patch_commit_message:
cookie: lowercase the domain names before PSL checks

Reported-by: Harry Sintonen

Closes #12387

new_lines: /* the PSL check requires lowercase domain name and pattern */

behavior_changes: Before the patch, domain names were used verbatim in psl_is_cookie_domain_acceptable, making the check case-sensitive.
After the patch, both the request domain and cookie domain are lowercased using Curl_strntolower before the PSL check, ensuring case-insensitive comparison.
If the lowercased domains match a PSL entry, the cookie is now rejected, preventing supercookies from being set for public suffixes like 'co.uk' when the cookie uses a different case (e.g., 'co.UK').
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Enforce that cookies are not set for domains that are public suffixes, regardless of case variations.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: The patch lowercases both the request domain and the cookie domain before passing them to the PSL check, ensuring case-insensitive matching and preventing bypass.

root_cause_analysis.summary: The function Curl_cookie_add passed domain names directly to psl_is_cookie_domain_acceptable without case normalization, allowing case-sensitivity mismatches to bypass PSL-based domain acceptability checks.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: Case-sensitive comparison of domain names against the Public Suffix List (PSL), which contains lowercase entries, allowed a mixed-case cookie domain (e.g., "co.UK") to evade detection as a public suffix.

CVE-2023-46218.vulnerability_description:
This flaw allows a malicious HTTP server to set "super cookies" in curl that
are then passed back to more origins than what is otherwise allowed or
possible. This allows a site to set cookies that then would get sent to
different and unrelated sites and domains.

It could do this by exploiting a mixed case flaw in curl's function that
verifies a given cookie domain against the Public Suffix List (PSL). For
example a cookie could be set with `domain=co.UK` when the URL used a lower
case hostname `curl.co.uk`, even though `co.uk` is listed as a PSL domain.

CVE-2023-46219.patch_commit_message:
fopen: create short(er) temporary file name

Only using random letters in the name plus a ".tmp" extension. Not by
appending characters to the final file name.

Reported-by: Maksymilian Arciemowicz

Closes #12388

new_lines: The dirslash() function breaks a null-terminated pathname string into
directory and filename components then returns the directory component up
to, *AND INCLUDING*, a final '/'.  If there is no directory in the path,
This function returns a pointer to malloc'ed memory.
The input path to this function is expected to have a file name part.
/* find the rightmost path separator, if any */
/* skip over all the path separators, if any */
/* if there was a directory, append a single trailing slash */
/* The temp file name should not end up too long for the target file

behavior_changes: Added dirslash() to extract directory from filename.
Temporary file name changed from '<filename>.<rand>..tmp' to '<dir>/<rand>.tmp', where <rand> is a 41-character alphanumeric string.
The length of the random component increased from 9 to 41 bytes to further ensure uniqueness.
Added error handling for directory extraction failure.
Memory management adjusted to free the directory string.
patch_intent_analysis.intended_security_property: Integrity and availability of the HSTS store; the HSTS file must not be accidentally removed or corrupted due to filesystem limitations.
patch_intent_analysis.summary: The patch decouples the temporary file name from the original file name by placing a short randomly-named file in the same directory, preventing path length overflow.

root_cause_analysis.summary: When saving HSTS data, the temporary file name was constructed by appending a random suffix and '.tmp' to the original file name. If the original file name was excessively long, the resulting path could exceed the filesystem's maximum length, potentially causing unexpected truncation or overwriting of the original file, leading to loss of all HSTS data.
root_cause_analysis.unsafe_mechanism: The temporary file path was built using aprintf("%s.%s.tmp", filename, randsuffix) where filename could be arbitrarily long, resulting in a path that might exceed OS limits and trigger undefined behavior, possibly deleting or corrupting the original HSTS file.

CVE-2023-46219.vulnerability_description:
When saving HSTS data to an excessively long file name, curl could end up
removing all contents, making subsequent requests using that file unaware of
the HSTS status they should otherwise use.
